mappings.rationaleAI: Intense emotional state involving physiological arousal and fight-or-flight responses, connecting to aggression as a specific human motive (A.2) and to the nature of emotion (B.1) and its facial, vocal, and postural manifestations (B.3).

Neurophysiologically grounded states covering classification theories and the nature of emotion (B.1), somatic theories (James-Lange, Cannon-Bard) addressing the role of the nervous system (B.2), and cross-cultural variation in emotional expression (B.3).
Biological, psychological, and sociocultural dimensions of sexual experience, directly relevant to sexual behavior and deviations as specific human motives (A.2).

Amygdala-mediated emotional state producing physiological arousal (B.2), fight-flight-freeze behavioral responses (B.3), sharing substrates with anxiety as a specific human motive (A.2), and illustrating the nature of emotion (B.1).

Psychoanalytic drive theory as a foundational motivation theory (A.1), libido and psychosexual development addressing specific human motives (A.2), and dream interpretation as a diagnostic tool for understanding transient states (C.2).
Awareness including wakefulness, self-awareness, and metacognition, with medical definitions distinguishing consciousness from coma, directly relevant to altered states of consciousness (C.3).
Scientific study encompassing motivation (A), emotion (B), and transient states affecting behavior (C), including biological, behaviorist, and cognitive approaches to each domain.
The Expression of the Emotions addresses the nature of emotion (B.1) and the evolutionary basis of facial, vocal, and postural emotional expression (B.3).

Intrinsically motivated activity serving cognitive development and emotional regulation, relevant to motivation theories emphasizing intrinsic drives (A.1) and situational factors in motivational states (A.3).
Acquisition of knowledge through conditioning and observation, connecting to reinforcement-based motivation theory and practical applications of motivation (A.4).

Transitional stage with identity formation and autonomy negotiation relevant to achievement motivation and anxiety (A.2), and peer relationship intensification as a situational factor in motivation (A.3).
Trance induction via drumming or psychoactive substances to enter altered states of consciousness, directly relevant to hypnosis and related states (C.3).
